references") | 4 months agoFeb 1, 2026 | | View all files | ## Repository files navigation # Cursor Docs Hook [Permalink: Cursor Docs Hook](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#cursor-docs-hook) Keeps project documentation consistent by running a Cursor Agent doc pass after edits. Records edited files per conversation and, when the agent stops, invokes the Cursor Agent CLI in the background to update `docs/INDEX.md` and module-level `INDEX.md` files if there were edits. [![Demo](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook/raw/main/.github/screenshot.png)](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook/blob/main/.github/screenshot.png) ## Features [Permalink: Features](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#features) - Records which files were edited in the current conversation (`afterFileEdit`) - Runs a documentation pass only when something was actually changed - Triggers on stop whenever there were edits in this run - Runs the doc agent in the background so the hook returns immediately - Configurable model and logging via `--model` and `--log` in the hook command - No extra runtime dependencies (Node.js only) ## Quick Installation [Permalink: Quick Installation](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#quick-installation) Install using the [cursor-hook](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-hook) CLI: ``` npx cursor-hook install beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook ``` ### Dependencies [Permalink: Dependencies](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#dependencies) The hook uses Node.js and TypeScript; everything is installed and compiled during setup: - Dependencies are installed with `npm install` - TypeScript is compiled with `npm run build` - The compiled script runs via `node dist/docs.js` - The CLI runs install and build silently ## Requirements [Permalink: Requirements](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#requirements) - **Cursor** with [Cursor CLI](https://cursor.com/docs/cli/using) on PATH (`cursor` or `cursor-agent`) - **Node.js** >= 18.0.0 and npm (for installing and compiling the hook) ## How It Works [Permalink: How It Works](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#how-it-works) 1. **After each file edit** (`afterFileEdit`): - The script records `conversation_id` and `file_path` in `.cursor/hooks/docs/state/edits.json` - Same conversation: appends the file to the list if not already present - New conversation: replaces state with the new conversation and file 2. **On stop** (`stop`): - If the state has at least one edited file for this conversation: - Clears the edits state (so we only run once per batch of edits) - Starts **Cursor Agent CLI** in the background with a doc-pass prompt - Otherwise does nothing - Agent output is appended to `.cursor/hooks/docs/agent.log` when `--log` is passed in the command ## INDEX.md Convention [Permalink: INDEX.md Convention](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#indexmd-convention) The doc-pass agent is instructed to: - **docs/INDEX.md** — Project overview and links to module-level INDEX.md files - **Functional roots** (e.g. `src/main`, `src/main/agent`, `src/renderer`, `scripts`) — An `INDEX.md` describing the module and what can be used - **Subfolders with substantial content** — Their own `INDEX.md` where useful Only missing or outdated content is created or updated; accurate content is left as is. ## Configuration [Permalink: Configuration](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#configuration) Pass options in the hook command: - **Model:**`--model ` (e.g. `.../docs.js --model claude-sonnet-4.5`). Default: `auto`. - **Logging:**`--log` — when set, agent stdout/stderr go to `.cursor/hooks/docs/agent.log`. Example in `cursor-hook.config.json`: ``` "stop": [\ {\ "command": "node $HOME/.cursor/hooks/docs/dist/docs.js --model claude-sonnet-4.5 --log"\ }\ ] ``` ## File Structure [Permalink: File Structure](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#file-structure) After installation (project install via cursor-hook), files are under the hook directory: **Project installation:** ``` .cursor/ ├── hooks.json (or cursor-hook.config.json) └── hooks/ └── docs/ ├── docs.ts # Main TypeScript script ├── utils.ts # Utilities (state, stdin) ├── types.ts # Type definitions ├── dist/ # Compiled JS (created during install) │ ├── docs.js │ ├── utils.js │ └── types.js ├── package.json ├── tsconfig.json ├── node_modules/ └── state/ # Created at runtime in project root, see below # Runtime state (in project root where Cursor runs): / └── .cursor/ └── hooks/ └── docs/ ├── state/ │ └── edits.json # conversation_id + list of edited files └── agent.log # Cursor Agent CLI output ``` **Global installation:** ``` ~/.cursor/ ├── hooks.json └── hooks/ └── docs/ ├── docs.ts ├── utils.ts ├── types.ts ├── dist/ ├── package.json ├── tsconfig.json └── node_modules/ ``` State and logs are still written under the **project root** (e.g. `CURSOR_PROJECT_DIR` or cwd): `/.cursor/hooks/docs/`. ## Manual Setup [Permalink: Manual Setup](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#manual-setup) 1. Clone or download this repository and copy the `hooks/docs` directory to `~/.cursor/hooks/docs` (global) or ensure it is present as `hooks/docs` in the project and referenced by cursor-hook. 2. Install and build: `cd ~/.cursor/hooks/docs && npm install && npm run build` (or `cd hooks/docs && npm install && npm run build` for project). 3. Configure hooks in `~/.cursor/hooks.json` or project `cursor-hook.config.json`: ``` { "version": 1, "hooks": { "afterFileEdit": [\ {\ "command": "node $HOME/.cursor/hooks/docs/dist/docs.js"\ }\ ], "stop": [\ {\ "command": "node $HOME/.cursor/hooks/docs/dist/docs.js"\ }\ ] } } ``` ## Testing [Permalink: Testing](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#testing) ``` # Test afterFileEdit (record an edit) echo '{ "conversation_id": "test-123", "hook_event_name": "afterFileEdit", "file_path": "/path/to/project/src/main/App.tsx" }' | node ~/.cursor/hooks/docs/dist/docs.js # Check state (run from project root; state is under .cursor/hooks/docs/state/) cat .cursor/hooks/docs/state/edits.json # Test stop with no trigger (no edits in state) echo '{ "conversation_id": "other-conv", "hook_event_name": "stop", "loop_count": 0 }' | node ~/.cursor/hooks/docs/dist/docs.js # Test stop with trigger (state has edits for same conversation_id) echo '{ "conversation_id": "test-123", "hook_event_name": "stop", "loop_count": 0 }' | node ~/.cursor/hooks/docs/dist/docs.js # Then check .cursor/hooks/docs/agent.log (when --log was passed) ``` ## Updating [Permalink: Updating](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#updating) ``` npx cursor-hook install beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook ``` ## Uninstallation [Permalink: Uninstallation](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#uninstallation) **Project installation:** - Remove the hook directory: `rm -rf .cursor/hooks/docs` (or wherever cursor-hook installed it). - Remove the `afterFileEdit` and `stop` entries for this hook from `cursor-hook.config.json` or `.cursor/hooks.json`. **Global installation:** - Remove the hook directory: `rm -rf ~/.cursor/hooks/docs`. - Edit `~/.cursor/hooks.json` and remove the `afterFileEdit` and `stop` entries for this hook. Optional: remove runtime state and logs: `rm -rf .cursor/hooks/docs` in the project root. ## License [Permalink: License](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#license) MIT ## Contributing [Permalink: Contributing](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#contributing) Pull requests are welcome. For larger changes, please open an issue first. ## Links [Permalink: Links](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#links) - [Cursor Hooks](https://cursor.com/docs/agent/hooks) - [Cursor CLI](https://cursor.com/docs/cli/using) - [cursor-hook](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-hook) — Develop and install hooks from Git repositories ## About Cursor Agent hook so project documentation stays consistent after edits. ### Topics [hooks](https://github.com/topics/hooks "Topic: hooks") [documentation](https://github.com/topics/documentation "Topic: documentation") [cursor](https://github.com/topics/cursor "Topic: cursor") [cursor-agent](https://github.com/topics/cursor-agent "Topic: cursor-agent") ### Resources [Readme](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook#readme-ov-file) ### Uh oh! There was an error while loading. [Please reload this page](https://github.com/beautyfree/cursor-docs-hook). [select all](https://forum.cursor.com/t/how-do-hooks-work-in-cursor-cli/150201) [cancel selecting](https://forum.cursor.com/t/how-do-hooks-work-in-cursor-cli/150201) [Jan 18](https://forum.cursor.com/t/how-do-hooks-work-in-cursor-cli/150201/1 "Jump to the first post") 1 / 2 Jan 18 [Jan 31](https://forum.cursor.com/t/how-do-hooks-work-in-cursor-cli/150201/2) ## post by Ilnur\_Ismagilov on Jan 18 [![](https://avatars.discourse-cdn.com/v4/letter/i/bc79bd/48.png)](https://forum.cursor.com/u/ilnur_ismagilov) [Ilnur Ismagilov](https://forum.cursor.com/u/ilnur_ismagilov) [Jan 18](https://forum.cursor.com/t/how-do-hooks-work-in-cursor-cli/150201 "Post date") It’s unclear for me how to use hooks. Fir example, grind loop based on stop event. This loop will be triggered any time I have any chat? If I’m just discussing in Cursor chat how to fix a complicated failed test it will still run failed tests and try to fix them 5 times? - [Cursor CLI (Jan 8, 2026)](https://forum.cursor.com/t/cursor-cli-jan-8-2026/148374/9) 340 views 1 link 13 days later ## post by condor on Jan 31 [![](https://sea3.discourse-cdn.com/cursor1/user_avatar/forum.cursor.com/condor/48/73148_2.png)](https://forum.cursor.com/u/condor) [Condor (Tee)](https://forum.cursor.com/u/condor)[condor](https://forum.cursor.com/u/condor) Community Developer [Jan 31](https://forum.cursor.com/t/how-do-hooks-work-in-cursor-cli/150201/2 "Post date") hi [@Ilnur\_Ismagilov](https://forum.cursor.com/u/ilnur_ismagilov) Cursor CLI does use hooks hooks to observe, control, and extend the agent loop with custom scripts. These hooks are spawned processes that communicate via stdin/stdout using JSON and can run before or after defined stages of the agent loop. They are triggered automatically: e.g. • `stop` \- on agent completion => when agent finishes the reply Hooks can be configured in `hooks.json` at project level (`/.cursor/hooks.json`) or globally (`~/.cursor/hooks.json`) [Hooks at Cursor Docs](https://cursor.com/docs/agent/hooks) For your specific issue you could use Cursor IDE since it has the Debug Mode already built in which is better for that task. web.description: Here we demonstrate how to use GitButler with the new Cursor Hooks functionality to automate creating branches for chat sessions and ...
web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/NOYK7LTFvZM/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [Using Cursor Hooks for automatic version control](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NOYK7LTFvZM)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [GitButler](https://www.youtube.com/@gitbutlerapp)
**Uploaded at**: 2025-09-29
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 04:10
**Views**: 3595
**Likes**: 34
**Category**: People & Blogs

## Description

```
Here we demonstrate how to use GitButler with the new Cursor Hooks functionality to automate creating branches for chat sessions and committing work as you go with smart commit messages. Never lose a step again.
```

## Transcript

Hi, I'm Scott, CEO of Gitler. Today
we're going to be talking about
integrating cursor and gitler. So if you
use cursor in the last release in 1.7
they released beta uh hooks uh for life
cycle integration. So what git bugler
can do now is you can tell cursor when
you hit these points in your life cycle
of doing sort of agentic AI work in
cursor use gitler to do the version
control parts. So, automatically create
commit messages, automatically create
commits after every task is is completed
and so on and so forth. So, I'm going to
show you how to set that up and how to
get it going so that you can use Gitler
as your version control tool for cursor
automatically. Okay, so let's say that
this is our cursor window. In the newest
version, you can say initialize user
level hooks in this sort of developer
tool. And what it's going to do is it's
going to write to your users.cursor
hooks.json directory. So, it's going to
put in this sort of generic thing that
has this example before prompt submit.
There's a whole bunch of different sort
of lifestyle hooks that you can you can
put in. What we're going to do is
replace it with these git other ones.
So, the ones that we want to do is the
git other CLI cursor after edit in the
after file edit and the stop hook in the
stop. I have d-nly in here only for the
nightly that I'm testing this on, but
you'll do it without this. And you can
read more about that in the gutler docs.
So now once I have that I'm going to ask
cursor to start doing some work for me
in some tasks. So let's say that I'm
trying to add some admin pages.
So we can see as it's running it's has
created a new branch. We can see that it
sort of has this session attached to it
that we're kind of watching what it
does. And as it's editing files it's
automatically assigning those files that
cursor is editing. We can see them
coming in here to that lane to that the
new branch that's created for this this
task. And now as soon as it's done, you
can see it create a new commit, rename
the commit, and then actually create a
new branch name if it's one of the
default branch names. And we can
actually go into this commit and see
that it also created a commit message
for us
and added all of our files to that. So
now let's keep working on this commit.
Let's say that I found something in it.
Let's see.
Okay, so let's say that I want to keep
working on this by saying, you know
what, this limit shouldn't be 50. This
limit should be 20. So let's ask cursor
to fix that for us.
And you can see it did this fairly
quickly, right? So it creates a new
commit with a sort of default message
and then it rewrites it with AI to be a
little bit better. So now we have our
change that we just asked it to do. So
every time that it finishes a task, it's
going to create a new commit. So let's
just be pedantic here.
This is maybe not the best way to edit
files, but you know, and you can see it
uses the prompt first and then it
rewrites it with AI. So now what we have
is we have sort of this thing that added
this feature. We have a sort of small
fix up commit here and then we have a
larger one. Now I can do a new task. I
actually don't have a read me in here.
So let's have cursor write this up for
me. And we can see that since I started
a new task, it created a new branch and
it wrote that into that new branch and
it names the branch and it and it does
its own commit message. So when I create
a new task, it'll create a new branch
and it'll try to put the changes into
that branch so that I can open them as
separate pull requests for example. Now
the last thing I'm going to do is take a
look at this branch. This is kind of a
silly set of commits, right? We create
something, we change it to 20, then we
change it to 30. Really what we want is
one commit that just has it be 30. So
what I can do is I can go ahead and
squash these together by dragging and
dropping them sort of all together. Or
if I go back, I could also do a squash
all commits here and just create sort of
one commit out of that. Now, it'll just
put all of the commit messages together.
So, we probably don't want that. So,
I'll go ahead and edit that. And then I
can go ahead and open my PR. And now, it
looks like I only had that 30 to begin
with. So, that's the new hooks
functionality where you can integrate
gitother and cursor. It's pretty easy to
just add that hooks.json. You can read
it in the git other docs. Um, and it
should just sort of automatically be
version controlling everything that
you're working on and and writing commit
messages and writing branch names and
doing everything necessary for that. So,
hope that was interesting. Later.

Editing raw JSON for your hooks configuration is tedious and error-prone. You have to remember exact hook names, required properties, and valid values—all without any guidance from your editor. There's a simple solution that transforms this experience completely.

This lesson shows you how to add a single configuration block to your user settings that enables IntelliSense for all your `hooks.json` files. You'll get autocomplete suggestions, inline documentation, validation warnings, and hover hints—making hook configuration fast, reliable, and pleasant.

### The Solution: `json.schemas`

By adding the following snippet to your user `settings.json`, you instruct Cursor (and VS Code) to download and apply a schema to any file named `hooks.json` within a `.cursor` directory.

This configuration enables several key features:

- **Autocomplete:** Get suggestions for valid hook names like `beforeReadFile` or `beforeSubmitPrompt`.
- **Validation:** See warnings for missing required properties (like `command`) or incorrect data types.
- **IntelliSense:** Hover over properties to see descriptions and expected values.

This small setup transforms hook configuration from tedious and error-prone to fast and reliable across all your projects.

### How it Works

1. **Open User Settings (JSON):** Use the command palette to open your global `settings.json` file.
2. **Add the Schema Configuration:** Paste the provided JSON block into your settings. This enables schema downloading and creates the file association.
3. **Start Using IntelliSense:** Open any `.cursor/hooks.json` file and start typing—autocomplete suggestions will appear immediately.

The schema used here is based on Cursor's documentation and hosted on `unpkg`. If Cursor releases an official schema in the future, you can simply update the URL.

```

{
  "json.schemaDownload.enable": true,
  "json.schemas": [\
    {\
      "fileMatch": [\
        ".cursor/hooks.json"\
      ],\
      "url": "https://unpkg.com/cursor-hooks/schema/hooks.schema.json"\
    }\
  ]
}
```
